sources.short: Funeral Oration on Judge Eben Newton by Dr. Wm Dickson, Nov 1885

open_questions.question: Is Isaac Newton Jr. the son of HD0154 (Capt. Isaac Newton b.1748)?
open_questions.notes: The funeral oration says Eben Newton's grandfather was 'a large landholder of Goshen with three sons: Enoch, Samuel, and Isaac.' H00148 (John Newton b.1726, Hooker descendant gen 5) had documented sons Samuel (HD0156) and Isaac (HD0154) — two of the three. H00148's research notes say he 'purchased many acres of wild land with son Isaac.' If HD0154 (Capt. Isaac Newton b.1748) is the Goshen landholder grandfather and Isaac Newton Jr. is his son, then Isaac Jr., John Newton (X01993), and Judge Eben Newton are all 6th/7th-generation Hooker descendants through H00148 → HD0007 → H00044 → H00012 → H00003 (Mary Hooker Newton) → Thomas Hooker. This would make HD2915-HD2924 double Hooker descendants through both the Thompson/Cowles line and the Newton/Hooker line. Needs primary source confirmation — check Goshen CT vital records and Newton genealogy.
open_questions.action: Check Goshen CT vital records; verify HD0154 children; consult Newton genealogy

cross_connections.display_label: Another Isaac Newton, same generation, born 4 years apart — probable cousins

narrative_blocks.header: Invalid fourteen years, one side paralyzed
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Newton spent the last fourteen years of his life as an invalid, with one side paralyzed and 'almost helpless.' The condition is consistent with either a stroke around 1798 or progressive degenerative disease in his fifties. He died in poverty in 1812 at Goshen Connecticut at age sixty-seven, his wife Rebecca Minott saving only fifty acres at his death — and even those were sold by the town selectmen in 1816-1817 for ten dollars an acre.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited father's property, lost it, died poor
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac came into possession of all his father Samuel Newton's property and lost it before his death — a complete inheritance-to-poverty reversal in a single lifetime. He fathered ten children with Rebecca Minott (married Jan 10, 1769) before his fourteen-year invalidism set in. The two-Isaac-Newton family network in central Connecticut — Isaac of Woodbridge born 1744 and Isaac of Durham born 1740, both Hooker descendants through Samuel and Mary Hooker Newton — were almost certainly known to each other.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Woodbridge 1744, baptized Parish of Amity
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Newton was born September 2, 1744 in Woodbridge Connecticut and was baptized the same day at the Parish of Amity. He descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker in the fifth generation through Mary Hooker Newton, his great-great-grandmother, by way of the Woodbridge Newton branch.

institutions.institution_blurb: Troy Female Seminary, attended 1836-1838 at age 17-19. Emma Hart Willard (H00597) founded the school; Almira Hart Phelps (H00598), another Hooker descendant, was assistant principal. Eunice also studied at the adjacent Rensselaer School under Amos Eaton — the lab training that directly produced her 1856 greenhouse effect paper.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants at Troy Seminary
cross_connections.description: Both Hooker descendants at Troy Seminary — Phelps as vice-principal, Foote as student who later published the first greenhouse effect paper.

cross_connections.display_label: Willard's Troy Seminary trained her in sciences
cross_connections.description: Emma Hart Willard founded the school where Foote received the scientific training that led to her 1856 greenhouse effect discovery.

cross_connections.display_label: another Newton scientist whose discoveries were credited elsewhere
another woman whose discovery a man was credited for
the next woman to publish in serious physics journals after her

notable.notable_blurb: First to describe the greenhouse effect, 1856

narrative_blocks.header: First paper on the greenhouse effect, 1856
narrative_blocks.body: In 1856, Eunice Newton Foote submitted a paper to the AAAS demonstrating that carbon dioxide absorbs significantly more heat than air or hydrogen, and theorizing that higher CO2 levels would raise the Earth's temperature. This was the first known scientific paper to identify the greenhouse effect. Her paper predated John Tyndall's 1859 work by three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Signed the 1848 Seneca Falls Declaration of Sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: Foote and her husband Elisha were both signatories of the Declaration of Sentiments at the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first gathering focused solely on women's rights. She was one of five women who prepared the proceedings for publication. Elizabeth Cady Stanton was her neighbor; Elisha had purchased the house that the Stanton family moved into in 1847.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied at Troy alongside cousin Almira Phelps
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Newton attended Troy Female Seminary from 1836 to 1838. During her attendance, Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps — Emma Willard's sister and another Thomas Hooker descendant — was assistant principal, teaching science and writing the botany textbooks used in class. Eunice also studied at the adjacent Rensselaer School under Amos Eaton, learning laboratory methods.

narrative_blocks.header: Her inventions patented in her husband's name
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Foote was a prolific inventor: a thermostatically controlled cooking stove (1842), a rubber boot insert (1860), and a paper-making machine (1864). She told Elizabeth Cady Stanton in 1868 that half of all patents filed were on women's inventions — but men took out the patents because women couldn't defend them in court. Her husband Elisha collected a substantial infringement settlement on the stove patent.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Wethersfield Foote line
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice's 1841 marriage to Elisha Foote folded her into the Foote family that had founded Wethersfield Connecticut in 1635. Her own Hooker descent ran through Mary Hooker and Roger Newton — but her husband's line ran back to Nathaniel Foote, shared with Almira Mills Foote and Eli Foote as the three Foote-line in-laws of the Hooker tree.

education.degree: Scientific and classical curriculum under Emma Willard

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband bought Richfield land from Tallmadge — both Hooker descendants

burial.plot_notes: d. Sep 13, 1813 Hudson, Summit County OH. Burial location unverified.

parents.father_research_notes: Isaac Newton Jr. (X01994) — probable son of Capt. Isaac Newton HD0154; see open_questions

cross_connections.display_label: Two John Newtons, same family, parallel Newton-Hooker branches.
Settled the Richfield Township Tallmadge had purchased for the Western Reserve.
Edwards bought the township; Newton settled it — both Hooker descendants.

open_questions.question: Is Dr. Secretary Rawson, who practiced medicine in Richfield 1824+ for 40 years, a relative of the Hooker-Rawson line?
open_questions.notes: Richfield Township history mentions 'Dr. Secretary Rawson' as Richfield's first physician from 1824, practicing 40 years. Known for temperance work, teaching at Richfield Academy, and founding the town's first library 1825. 'Secretary' is an unusual given name. The Hooker-Rawson line has many gen 5-6 members (HD0299 Nathan Rawson, HD0304 Stephen Rawson, HD0305 Asa Rawson b.1775) — a physician among them migrating to Ohio is plausible. Needs: Rawson genealogy search for any physician named Secretary or with a name that could be a nickname; Richfield Academy records; Ohio medical licensing records 1820s.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Hooker descendant for double-line children
narrative_blocks.body: He married Laura Hooker Thompson in 1810 — both bride and groom were 6th-generation descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker through entirely separate lines. Their children descend doubly: through the Newton-Hooker line and through the Hooker-Cowles line.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut farmer who carved out an Ohio town
narrative_blocks.body: He removed from Goshen, Connecticut to Richfield Township in Summit County, Ohio sometime before the 1820s. By that decade he had purchased most of the land around Richfield's east center, building the homestead later owned by Homer Carter.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought his widowed mother west to Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Rebecca Minott Newton lived with him in Richfield in her widowhood before moving on to live with his brother Eben in Canfield, Ohio. Both sons had migrated to the Western Reserve.

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband was his great-great-great-grandson — confirmed double Hooker

bio.notes: Middle name "Hooker" explicitly honors the Hooker ancestry through her mother Pamelia Cowles

narrative_blocks.header: Enoch died young
narrative_blocks.body: Enoch died in 1794, aged about two.

sources.short: Funeral Oration on Judge Eben Newton by Dr. Wm Dickson, Nov 1885

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Rep. Ohio 1851-53; Ohio Senate; judge

cross_connections.display_label: Twenty-year law partner — Whittlesey & Newton, Canfield OH 1821-1841

narrative_blocks.header: From Goshen CT farm boy to U.S. Congressman
narrative_blocks.body: Eben Newton left Goshen, Connecticut at age 19 with nothing, arrived in Portage County, Ohio in 1814, and worked agricultural land while studying law. Admitted to the bar in 1823, he practiced in Canfield for decades, served as presiding judge of the court of common pleas 1844-1851, and was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives as a Whig in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-year law partnership with Elisha Whittlesey
narrative_blocks.body: Newton formed a law partnership with Elisha Whittlesey that lasted twenty years — the firm Whittlesey & Newton, Canfield OH, 1821-1841. Whittlesey was one of the founders of the Whig party, served as public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve, and was later appointed first Comptroller of the U.S. Treasury by Presidents Harrison, Pierce, and Lincoln, becoming known as 'the watch dog of the treasury.' Newton also served as president of the Ashtabula & New Lisbon Railroad 1856-1859.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought his mother west in a covered wagon
narrative_blocks.body: In 1819, having not heard from his mother Rebecca Minot Newton in five years, Eben returned to Connecticut to find her. He located her in Woodbury, living with a half sister — she failed to recognize him when he arrived. He loaded her household goods into a cheap second-hand wagon and drove her the long journey west to Ohio, where she lived first with brother John in Richfield, then with Eben in Canfield until her death.

open_questions.question: Was Eben Newton's 20-year law partner Elisha Whittlesey a Hooker descendant through the Whittlesey-Newton line?
open_questions.notes: Elisha Whittlesey (b.1783 Southbury CT) studied law under Tapping Reeve at Litchfield CT, then partnered with Eben Newton for 20 years in Canfield OH. Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097, gen 5 Hooker descendant) lived in Litchfield County and had children born 1775-1799 in Southbury CT. Elisha's birth year fits the sequence. If Elisha is HD0097's son, Newton's closest professional collaborator for 20 years was also a gen 6 Hooker descendant — neither man knowing of the shared ancestry. Note: Tapping Reeve (X00614, Litchfield law school) is already in the Hooker project through his marriage to Sally Burr. Both Newton and Whittlesey passed through his school.
open_questions.resolution: Elisha Whittlesey is NOT a Hooker descendant. He descends from John Whittlesey of Saybrook through a different branch (Whittlesey book #310, son of John #123). He did NOT study under Tapping Reeve — he studied law in his brother Matthew Beale Whittlesey's office in Danbury CT. The firm Whittlesey & Newton, Canfield OH is confirmed 1821-1841. Elisha is documented as orbit_non_descendant (X01997).

cross_connections.display_label: His great-great-grandson married a Hooker descendant — confirmed double Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Whole family entered the church covenant together
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, Samuel and Phebe were admitted to full communion in the Milford church — on the very same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Newton cousins married two Platt sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Phebe Platt in Milford; his first cousin Fletcher Newton married Phebe's sister Hannah Platt. Two Newton cousins took two Platt sisters — the families of Milford interlacing through a single generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His first cousin — both married Platt sisters from the same Milford family.

narrative_blocks.header: The whole family entered the church covenant
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, she and Samuel were admitted to full communion in the Milford church on the same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister Hannah married her husband's first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe married Samuel Newton; her sister Hannah married Samuel's first cousin Fletcher Newton. Two Platt sisters wed two Newton first cousins — the families of Milford knit together across one generation.
